name: Air France Flight 447
description: Air France Flight 447 (AF447) was a scheduled international passenger flight from Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, to Paris, France, that crashed into the Atlantic Ocean on June 1, 2009, killing all 228 people on board. While the official cause, determined after a lengthy investigation, points to a confluence of factors including pilot error (inadequate stall recovery techniques), faulty airspeed sensors (pitot tubes), and adverse weather conditions, the event has spawned various conspiracy theories and alternative explanations. Some theories suggest foul play, ranging from terrorist acts and sabotage to government cover-ups and even extraterrestrial involvement. These theories often cite perceived inconsistencies in the official report, questioning the reliability of the black box data or highlighting the perceived lack of transparency in the investigation. Proponents of these theories often point to the fact that the wreckage was not located for nearly two years as evidence of a cover-up. They also scrutinize the actions of the pilots and the airline's procedures. The mainstream perspective, supported by the BEA (Bureau d'Enquêtes et d'Analyses pour la sécurité de l'aviation civile), France's air accident investigation authority, firmly attributes the crash to technical malfunction combined with human error. The BEA's reports detail the pilots' misinterpretation of airspeed data and their incorrect reactions to the stall warning, exacerbated by turbulence. Critics of the conspiracy theories emphasize the thoroughness of the official investigation, the extensive data recovered from the flight recorders, and the lack of credible evidence supporting alternative explanations. They argue that the complex nature of the accident, with multiple contributing factors, can be difficult to comprehend for the general public, leading to speculation and mistrust.
subtitle: Crash in the Atlantic: Theories and Facts

claims.claim: The crash was caused by a bomb planted on board.
claims.evidence: Fragments of metal with unusual characteristics were found. Some believe these indicate an explosion.
claims.criticism: The official BEA report attributes the damage to the impact with the ocean surface and subsequent underwater corrosion. No explosive residue was found.

claims.claim: The French government is covering up the true cause of the crash to protect Airbus or Air France.
claims.evidence: The delay in finding the wreckage and the perceived reluctance to fully disclose all information are cited as evidence.
claims.criticism: The delay in locating the wreckage was due to the vastness and depth of the search area. The BEA published comprehensive reports detailing all aspects of the investigation.

claims.claim: Extraterrestrial involvement or some other unexplained phenomenon caused the crash.
claims.evidence: Some cite witness accounts or unusual radar anomalies near the flight path.
claims.criticism: These claims lack verifiable evidence and are based on speculation and misinterpretation of data. Mainstream science offers no support for such theories.

keyFigures.name: Jean-Pierre Otelli
keyFigures.role: Author of books questioning the official report.

keyFigures.name: Alain Bouillard
keyFigures.role: Chief Investigator of the BEA for AF447

mythologyConnections: The Atlantic Ocean as a site of lost civilizations (Atlantis)
Icarus myth (failure due to technological hubris)

timeline.event: Air France Flight 447 crashes into the Atlantic Ocean.
The flight recorders are recovered from the ocean floor.
The BEA releases its final report, citing pilot error and faulty equipment.
